engines_involved.role: primary — auto-creates triage alert from any threat signal
conditional — only fires when severity is critical or high
best-effort — logs a risk entry for every threat regardless of severity

current_state.summary: Three-engine fan-out is fully wired. AlertTriageEngine.ingest_alert() exists and is called unconditionally. IncidentOrchestrationEngine.create_incident() is guarded correctly behind severity in (critical, high). RiskRegisterEngine.create_risk() runs best-effort for all severities. All three engine files exist on disk.
current_state.severity_normalisation: Severity is validated against {critical, high, medium, low, info} before use; unknown values fall back to 'medium'. IncidentOrchestrationEngine re-validates severity against {critical, high, medium, low} with fallback 'high'.
current_state.isolation: _safe_call() wraps each downstream call so one failure does not abort the chain.
current_state.event_bus_wiring: Registered on both TrustGraph bus (string keys) and legacy async EventBus. Legacy bus uses EventType enum. TrustGraph bus.subscribe() is synchronous; emit() is async — subscriber is called synchronously from async context.

what_works: Alert auto-creation fires for every THREAT_DETECTED event regardless of severity.
Incident auto-creation correctly gates on critical/high severity only.
Risk register entry created for every threat — risk_category='operational', likelihood/impact mapped from severity.
Severity normalisation prevents invalid enum values from reaching downstream engines.
_safe_call isolation prevents one broken engine from silently killing the chain.
Both event bus paths (TrustGraph + legacy) are registered at app startup via register_cross_category_subscribers() called at app.py:5011.
what_doesnt: No deduplication — repeated THREAT_DETECTED events for the same entity_id generate duplicate alerts, incidents, and risk entries on every emission.
No correlation back to the originating alert — the created alert_id is returned from _create_alert() but never stored or linked to the incident created in step 2.
entity_id is passed in raw_alert_json but AlertTriageEngine has no foreign-key relationship to the emitting engine's record.
Risk entry uses hardcoded risk_category='operational' — there is no mapping from entity_type to an appropriate category.
RISK_ASSESSED event is NOT emitted after the risk entry is created, so downstream risk posture recalculation never triggers.
Incident type is hardcoded 'other' — no mapping from entity_type (e.g. 'malware', 'lateral_movement') to a meaningful incident type.
on_risk_assessed handler is a placeholder (logger.debug only) — the risk register write does not cascade to posture scoring.
TrustGraph bus.emit() is async but subscribers registered via bus.subscribe() are called synchronously — awaiting is not guaranteed in all execution paths.
No test in the Beast Mode suite exercises the full 3-step fan-out end-to-end.
acceptance_criteria: AC1: Given a THREAT_DETECTED event with severity='critical', AlertTriageEngine, IncidentOrchestrationEngine, and RiskRegisterEngine each receive exactly one call with correct org_id scoping.
AC2: Given a THREAT_DETECTED event with severity='low', only AlertTriageEngine and RiskRegisterEngine are called; IncidentOrchestrationEngine must NOT be called.
AC3: Given an invalid severity value (e.g. 'extreme'), AlertTriageEngine receives severity='medium' and IncidentOrchestrationEngine is NOT called.
AC4: The alert_id returned by AlertTriageEngine is stored on the event payload so callers can retrieve it; it must also be linked to any auto-created incident.
AC5: Duplicate suppression — if an identical (org_id, entity_id, source_engine) combination fires within 60 seconds, subsequent THREAT_DETECTED events must not create new alert/incident/risk entries.
AC6: RISK_ASSESSED event is emitted after the risk entry is created, carrying the new risk_id and org_id.
AC7: A pytest integration test in tests/test_cross_category_flows.py covers the full fan-out for both critical and low severity paths.
AC8: Failure of any one downstream engine (simulated by mocking to raise an exception) must not prevent the other two from being called.
